name: Fort Washakie
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in central Wyoming's basin and range country. Hunt for agate, jasper, and chalcedony on public land with no permit required.
description:
Fort Washakie sits on BLM land in Fremont County's high desert terrain, offering casual rockhounders the chance to search for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens without requiring permits or fees. The area lies at nearly 5,900 feet elevation on relatively flat to gently rolling sagebrush benchland typical of central Wyoming's broad intermontane basins.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range tectonic province, where Eocene to Oligocene volcanic activity and subsequent alluvial deposition created favorable conditions for silica-rich minerals. Agate nodules and chalcedony outcrops occur scattered across eroded hillsides and in washes where water has exposed fresh material. Jasper specimens, often banded or brecciated, weather out along benches and ridgetops.

This location appeals to beginner and intermediate rockhounders seeking public land access without the structure or cost of a fee dig. Material typically occurs as hand-sized pieces scattered on the surface or accessible through light surface collecting. The relatively flat terrain makes it accessible compared to more mountainous Wyoming rockhounding sites.

Visitors should respect the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA regulations and avoid any disturbing activity on this sovereign Shoshone Nation trust land boundary area. The remote setting offers quiet collecting opportunities but requires self-sufficiency regarding water and supplies.

Best results come from working old stream channels, eroded hillsides, and rocky benches where specimens naturally concentrate. Early morning collecting often yields better results before afternoon winds kick up dust.
history: This region sits within the Basin and Range province where Eocene volcanic silica-rich fluids and subsequent alluvial processes deposited agate and jasper nodules that formed during cooling and mineralization of ancient hydrothermal systems. The modern landscape reflects millions of years of basin subsidence and erosional unroofing that continuously exposes new specimens.
tips: Focus your search on eroded stream washes and rocky benches where water action has naturally concentrated and exposed specimens. Look for agate with banding patterns or color zoning in earth tones from cream to rust, and chalcedony that appears translucent or waxy. Bring a 5-gallon bucket, small rock hammer, and hand lens to inspect material on the spot. Work early morning hours before afternoon dust storms reduce visibility and make the terrain harder to read.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling sagebrush benchland at 5,867 feet elevation. Terrain is rocky with scattered outcrops and eroded washes. Footing is stable on compacted earth but watch for loose rock and hidden holes in sagebrush areas. Walking distance from parking is variable depending on where you position yourself; typically accessible within 100-300 yards of pullouts.
written_directions: From Lander, Wyoming, head northwest on WY 137 toward Fort Washakie for approximately 15 miles. Turn east onto BLM access roads near the fort area. GPS coordinates 43.131821, -108.97879 mark the general collecting zone. Look for pullouts along dirt tracks on the north and east sides of the benchland. No specific marked trailhead; use terrain features and rock outcrops as navigation references.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel BLM roads in fair condition. High-clearance helpful but not essential for main access points. Washboard surface typical. Passable year-round except during spring mud season.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM dirt roads. No developed lot. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical pullout locations. Park away from active vehicle tracks.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. No removal of rock in place; surface collecting only. Respect closure notices and tribal land boundaries adjacent to site.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Surface collecting only
Respect nearby tribal land boundaries
nearest_services: Lander, Wyoming, approximately 15 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved BLM roads require high-clearance vehicles but collecting areas are walkable on stable ground. No wheelchair access due to uneven terrain and lack of developed paths. Sturdy footwear recommended to avoid sharp rock and hidden sagebrush holes.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding near Fort Washakie, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Fort Washakie BLM land produces agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Agate typically shows banding in cream to rust tones, while jasper appears as brecciated or banded pieces. Chalcedony occurs as translucent waxy material, often in nodular form.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect agate at Fort Washakie?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land at Fort Washakie. You must follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA regulations and collect for personal use only. Commercial collecting is prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Fort Washakie?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best conditions. Summer provides warm, stable weather ideal for accessing BLM roads and spending full days in the field. Avoid spring mud season (March-April) when roads become impassable, and winter months when snow may limit access.

faq.question: Is Fort Washakie rockhounding site beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Fort Washakie is ideal for beginners. The terrain is relatively flat and accessible, material is found on the surface requiring no excavation, and there are no permits or fees. The 25-pound daily limit encourages responsible collecting while building skills.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to Fort Washakie?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket for specimens, small rock hammer, hand lens for inspection, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and plenty of water. A GPS unit or smartphone with offline maps is helpful given the remote location. Pack first aid supplies and tell someone your planned collecting area.

faq.question: Can I bring my dogs rockhounding at Fort Washakie, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on BLM land at Fort Washakie. Keep them under control and bring extra water for them in the high desert heat. Be aware of wildlife including snakes and watch for hazards like sharp rock and loose terrain.

meta_description: Hunt agate, jasper, and chalcedony on BLM land near Fort Washakie, Wyoming. No permit required, 25 lbs daily limit. Beginner-friendly collecting.
